Week 2
Study Guide

Four Pillars of EAM Diagnosis · Eight Principles Patterns
Introduction to Tongue & Pulse Diagnosis
Prof. Daniel Schrier, D.O.M., L.Ac.
EAM310 Week 2 Quiz 2 Prep Summer 2026
Jonathan Centeno · D.AcHM Candidate · VUIM 2026–2030
TCM for Ballroom Dancers | Dance Medicine Specialty
1Context — EAM Diagnosis Fundamentals

Week 2 builds on the Eight Principles from Week 1 and zooms in on the two most tactile clinical skills: tongue and pulse observation. This guide covers everything from the transcript and slides — including the Eight Principles pattern manifestations that are testable on Quiz 2.

Professor Lin Peixiang — Opening Quote

"As for the content of the Yellow Emperor's Classic, if you fully understand even one sentence, you will be able to receive nourishment from that for your entire life."

💡 Quiz 2 High-Yield Targets

Four Pillars count & Chinese names · Eight Principles pattern manifestations (Full/Empty Heat/Cold) · Seven systems — which is the foundation · Three reasoning types · Tongue map zones (Schrier: "I will give you this on a quiz question") · Three tongue layers · Seasonal pulses (Schrier: "good quiz question — wink wink") · Cun/Guan/Chi organ assignments · Medication tongue effects
2The Four Pillars 四診 Sì Zhěn

These four methods run simultaneously — by the time a patient sits down, Observation has already begun.

⚠ The Count Trap — Schrier Flagged This as Testable

"Observation, auscultation & olfaction, inquiry, palpation — wait, that's five." No. Hearing AND Smelling are one pillar 聞 Wén. The answer is always four.
PillarChinesePinyinWhat It InvolvesKey Clinical Note
Observation望WàngVisual — spirit (Shén), complexion, tongue, posture, constitutionBegins before patient speaks. Don't touch the tongue — that's "just weird."
Hearing & Smelling聞WénVoice strength, cough, breathing; ask about odorsONE pillar. You ask about odor — you don't smell the stool directly.
Inquiry問WènAsk — not interrogate. 10 Traditional Questions + 16 Questions (Week 7)"We're not the CIA." Be curious: "Tell me how this shows up for you."
Palpation切QiēPulse (primary). Also abdominal, channel, point tendernessTongue is NOT palpation — it's observation. Pulse requires live contact.

🩰 Ballroom Dance Medicine Connection

In dance medicine, your Observation begins the moment a dancer walks in — posture asymmetries, where they guard or collapse, whether they're "performing" vs genuinely present. Palpation (pulse) on a high-level competitor post-performance will show a very different baseline than in clinic. Always note context: recent physical exertion, emotional state, dehydration (common in competitors) all shift the tongue and pulse significantly.
3Seven Systems of Pattern Differentiation

Eight Principles is the foundation — it applies in every case. The others build on top of it:

SystemScopeWhen Used
① Eight Principles 八纲 ★ FOUNDATIONExt/Int · Cold/Heat · Def/Excess · Yin/Yang — universally applicableAlways, first
② Qi, Blood & Body FluidsVital substance imbalances — Qi def, Blood stasis, Phlegm, etc.Daily clinical
③ Zang-Fu OrgansInternal organ pattern diagnosis — the workhorse of modern practiceMost common
④ Channels & Collaterals12 primary, sinew, divergent, luo, 8 extraordinaryAcupuncture
⑤ Six Stages (Shang Han)Zhang Zhong Jing's cold-damage framework — Taiyang → JueyinCold damage
⑥ Four Levels (Wen Bing)Ye Tian Shi's warm febrile system — Wei → Qi → Ying → XueWarm diseases
⑦ Three BurnersUpper/Middle/Lower Jiao — used in Wen Bing and Zang-FuSupplemental
4Critical Reasoning in EAM
TypeDirectionExample
DeductiveGeneral rule → specific patientFever + chills + floating pulse → assume exterior; then verify full constellation
InductiveObserved patterns → general ruleHistorically: floating pulse = exterior in most cases — becomes a working rule (but not absolute)
AnalogicalA causes B → A predicts B will developLong-term Heat damages Blood → opens vector for Internal Wind

🩺 The Floating Pulse Trap — Most Important Pearl This Week

Floating + exterior: simultaneous fever & chills, stiff neck, runny nose.   Floating + Yin deficiency: night sweats, five-palm heat, malar flush, afternoon fever, dry throat at night. Always put a "?" next to floating pulse until you have the full constellation. Cold limbs + hot torso = likely Qi stagnation blocking warmth, not a Cold pattern.
5Five-Phase Correspondences — Testable Items
Salsa · Vibes · Make · Smooth · Bachata
Tissue mnemonic — Wood · Fire · Earth · Metal · Water
S = Sinews (Wood/LV) V = Vessels (Fire/HT) M = Muscles (Earth/SP) S = Skin & Hair (Metal/LU) B = Bones (Water/KD)
ElementYin / Yang OrganColorEmotionSense OrganTissueSeasonPathogen
WoodLV / GBGreenAngerEyesSinewsSpringWind
FireHT / SI / PC / SJRedJoyTongueVesselsSummerHeat
EarthSP / STYellowPensivenessMouthMusclesLate SummerDampness
MetalLU / LIWhiteGriefNoseSkin & HairAutumnDryness
WaterKD / BLBlack/BlueFearEarsBonesWinterCold
5bEight Principles — Pattern Manifestations (Slides Ch.30)

These four pattern pairs come directly from Schrier's slides. Know them for clinical application — they are the core of Eight Principles diagnosis.

Full Heat vs Empty Heat
FeatureFull Heat (Excess Yang)Empty Heat (Yin Deficiency)
CauseExcess Yang — external invasion, internal generation, transformation from ColdYin deficiency — overwork (main cause), long-term Full Heat consuming Yin
FaceWhole face redMalar flush (cheeks only)
FeverHigh, all dayLow-grade, afternoon or evening
SweatingProfuseNight sweats
ThirstDesire to drink cold water (large amounts)Desire to drink in small sips
PainWorse with pressureNo abdominal pain
TongueRed, thick yellow coatingRed, little or no coating (peeled)
PulseRapid, Full (forceful)Floating-Empty, Rapid
Tx PrincipleClear HeatNourish Yin, clear Empty-Heat
Full Cold vs Empty Cold
FeatureFull Cold (Excess Yin)Empty Cold (Yang Deficiency)
CauseExcess Yin — external Cold invasion, internal Dampness/Phlegm, Cold foodsYang deficiency — overwork, excess Cold foods, damaged by Full Cold long-term
FaceBright-whiteDull-white (no lustre)
PainSharp, worse with pressureDull, better with pressure and warmth
BowelsBetter after bowel movementWorse after bowel movement
TonguePale, thick white coatingPale, swollen, wet, thin white coating
PulseFull-Tight-Deep (沉紧有力)Weak-Slow-Deep (沉迟无力)
Tx PrincipleWarm Interior, expel ColdWarm and tonify Yang

🩺 The Most-Tested Distinction: Pressure Response

Full patterns: pain/discomfort worse with pressure (pathogen resists). Deficiency patterns: pain better with pressure (emptiness wants support). This applies to both Cold and Heat patterns — it's the universal Excess/Deficiency differentiator.
6Tongue Diagnosis Introduction 舌診 Shé Zhěn

Part of Observation (望 Wàng). You look — never touch. Tongue changes slowly (days to weeks), reflecting the chronic underlying pattern. Cannot be faked.

The Tongue Map — Prof. Schrier's Version

⚠ Schrier's Exact Words: "I Will Give You This on a Quiz Question"

"Heart at the tip, Lung right behind, Liver/GB on the sides, Spleen/Stomach in the center, Kidney/Bladder/Intestines at the rear. These are things you DO need to know."
ZoneOrgan(s)Key Clinical Sign
TipHeart 心Red tip = Heart-Fire
Just behind tipLung 肺Swollen front = Phlegm in Lungs
SidesLiver / GB 肝胆Red sides = LV-Fire
Center ovalSpleen / Stomach 脾胃Stomach crack = ST-Yin def
Root / BackKD · BL · Intestines 肾膀胱Rootless coat = KD-Yin def
Three Diagnostic Layers
LayerWhat It ReflectsDepth
Coat 苔 TāiYang organs (esp. Stomach) · pathogenic factors · digestive strength. Coat = Wei/Defensive QiMost superficial
Body 體 TǐYin organs · Blood · Nutritive Qi (Ying). Body color = the single most reliable indicator of the true underlying condition.Middle — Ying level
Sublingual VeinsYuan (Original) Qi · Blood stasis (dark, distended, tortuous)Deepest — Yuan level
Five Things to Observe + Accuracy Factors
ObservationWhat You Look ForKey Clinical Significance
Body ColorNormal = pale red · Pale · Red · Deep Red · Purple · Blue-purplePale=Yang/Blood def · Red=Heat · Purple=Blood Stasis · Deviated=Internal Wind
Body ShapeSwollen · Thin · Stiff · Deviated · Toothmarks · Cracked · Short · LongShort=Cold · Long=Heat · Toothmarks=SP-Qi def · Deviated=Internal Wind
CoatingColor: white/yellow/grey/black · Thickness: thin/thick · Quality: moist/dry/greasy/peeledWhite=Cold · Yellow=Heat · Thick=pathogen · No coat=Yin def
MoistureWet · Moist · Dry · PeeledReflects Body Fluid status. Dry=Heat or Yin def. Wet=Yang def/Cold/Damp
Spirit 神Fresh & lustrous vs dull/dry ("cow tongue at the kosher deli")Spirit = Stomach-Qi. Good spirit = good prognosis even in serious disease.
Accuracy FactorEffect on Tongue Assessment
LightingMust use natural light (window). Artificial light distorts color — impossible to accurately assess under fluorescent classroom light
Diet & drinksCoffee, tea, colored food, smoking all throw off coat color. Nothing coloring 30 min before assessment
Technique5–10 seconds maximum (Schrier: "15–20 is already too long"). Relaxed protrusion — not forced. Repeat if needed.
SequenceInspect tip → middle → root (or root → middle → front). Then body color, then coating.
AgeChildren: generally redder. Elderly: drier, paler, more contracted — this is normal, not pathology
SeasonTongue shifts through seasons — summer slightly redder, winter slightly paler. Factor in on borderline findings.
Medication Effects on Tongue — All 7 (From Slides)
MedicationTongue EffectClinical Pearl
AntibioticsPeeled patchesLooks like Stomach-Yin def. If no Yin def signs → ask about meds. Instant credibility.
CorticosteroidsRed, swollen; tip much redderMimics Damp-Heat or Full-Heat
BronchodilatorsRed tip only — after many yearsLong-term inhalers → Heart/Lung heat at tip
DiureticsPeeling — similar to antibioticsDries Body Fluids → Stomach/Yin deficiency appearance
Anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs)Red points on surfaceLong-term NSAIDs generate internal Heat
Antineoplastic (chemo)Brown, thick coatingChemotherapy toxicity — distinguish from Toxic-Heat
Pepto-BismolBlack tongueHarmless: bismuth + oral sulfur. Ask immediately. Mystery solved.
7Pulse Diagnosis Introduction 脈診 Mài Zhěn

The deepest, most subjective, most rewarding skill in EAM. Week 2 is only the beginning — feel without judgment, describe what you notice.

Pulse Positions — From the Slides (Page 19 of 29)

The Nàn Jīng 難經 established taking the pulse at the radial artery divided into three areas at three different levels. The three sections are: cùn 寸/inch (front) · guān 関/gate (middle) · chǐ 尺/foot (rear).

CUN 寸 (index)
GUAN 关 (middle)
CHI 尺 (ring)
L
HT / PC 心包 · Upper Burner
LV / GB 肝胆 · Middle Burner
KD-Yin 肾阴 · Lower Burner
R
LU / LI 肺大肠 · Upper Burner
SP / ST 脾胃 · Middle Burner
KD-Yang 肾阳 · Lower Burner
Methods for Taking Pulse (From Slides)
  • Time: Classically early morning (Yin calm, Yang not yet extended). Practically: whenever your patient arrives.
  • Arm position: Patient's arm horizontal, NOT higher than the level of the heart
  • Placement: Index, middle, ring fingers simultaneously on the radial artery
  • Arrangement: Spread or press fingers together depending on patient's arm size
  • Regulating: Fingertip pads (not tips) contact the artery; middle finger (longest) is slightly contracted
  • Using the fingers: Ring finger is slightly more sensitive than the others — account for this when comparing positions
  • Sequence: Overall read first → then each position individually
Normal Pulse — Three Qualities + Seasonal Variation ★
QualityFeelReflects
Stomach-Qi 胃气Gentle, calm, unhurriedDigestive function intact — always present in a living person
Spirit 神Soft but with strength; regular rhythm; "alive"Vitality of the Mind — good spirit = good prognosis
Root 根Clearly felt deep, especially at Chi (rear position)Kidney Essence intact — the constitutional root

⚠ Schrier Flagged This as a Quiz Question: Seasonal Pulse Qualities

"Wiry pulse of the spring, flooding pulse of the summer, hair-like of the autumn, stone-like deep of the winter. Good quiz question — wink wink nudge nudge."
SeasonPulse QualityDescriptionWhy
Spring 春Wiry 弦 XiánTaut like a bowstringWood/Liver — Yang rising, life pushing up. Tense and taut like new growth.
Summer 夏Flooding 洪 HóngSurges like a floodFire — full Yang, everything expanded. Bigger, flooded in nature.
Autumn 秋Hair-like 毛 MáoSuperficial, delicateMetal — Yang contracting inward. Light at the surface, withdrawing.
Winter 冬Stone-like / Deep 石 ShíDeep, hard to findWater — full Yin, everything contracts and hides. Yang retreats deep.
8Journal Assignment — All 50 Entries Due by Week 9
RequirementDetails
Total50 entries (tongue + pulse per entry). Schrier: "Do more than 50 if you can."
PeopleMinimum 5 different people, each seen 4–5+ times. Track how things change over time.
RecordTongue: color, coat, moisture, shape, features. Pulse: rate, depth, strength, quality at all 6 positions.
Format+ / - symbols, descriptive metaphors, or drawings. "I don't know what I feel here" is completely valid.
ContextAlways note: time of day, recent meals, sleep, emotional state — all affect the readings.
9Quiz 2 Prep — Know These Cold
TopicThe Exact Answer
Four Pillars countAlways FOUR — Hearing & Smelling are ONE pillar (聞 Wén). 望聞問切.
Tongue mapTip=Heart · Behind tip=Lung · Sides=LV/GB · Center=SP/ST · Root=KD/BL/Intestines
Three tongue layersCoat=most superficial/Yang/pathogen · Body=Yin/Ying/true condition · Sublingual=Yuan Qi
Full vs Empty HeatFull: thick yellow coat + forceful pulse. Empty: NO coat + floating-empty thready pulse
Full vs Empty ColdFull: worse with pressure. Empty: better with pressure and warmth
Seasonal pulses ★Spring=Wiry · Summer=Flooding · Autumn=Hair-like · Winter=Stone-like/Deep
Floating pulse trap≠ always exterior. Also = Yin def (night sweats, five-palm heat, malar flush, dry throat at night)
Pulse positionsL: CUN=HT/PC · GUAN=LV/GB · CHI=KD-Yin. R: CUN=LU/LI · GUAN=SP/ST · CHI=KD-Yang
Medication → tongueAntibiotics=peeled · Steroids=red+swollen · Bronchodilators=red tip · Diuretics=peeling · NSAIDs=red points · Chemo=brown thick · Pepto=black
Short vs long tongueShort=Cold (contracts) · Long=Heat (expands)
Deviated tongue=Internal Wind (Liver). Not pathological if jaw was injured.
5-phase tissuesSVMSB — Salsa Vibes Make Smooth Bachata — Sinew/Vessels/Muscles/Skin/Bone
